lead: When you ring or email us for a quote, the detail you give shapes how accurate that first figure can be. Since 2005 our family-run team here in Westerfield has handled thousands of enquiries, and we have found that the ones with a little forethought lead to quicker, fairer quotes and far smoother moving days. You do not need to write an essay. You simply need to tell us the right things. Here is what to include when asking for a removals quote, and why each detail genuinely matters.

sections.h: Your Addresses and Where You're Really Moving
sections.body:
The first thing to tell us is where you are moving from and where you are moving to. That sounds obvious, but the addresses do more than tell us the distance. They tell us about the roads, the likely parking, and the kind of property at each end, all of which feed into a realistic quote. A move within Ipswich behaves very differently from a move out to a coastal village or across the county line, and the postcodes give us a head start before we have even spoken about the contents.

If you do not yet have a firm destination, that is fine, and it happens more often than people expect. Plenty of folk get in touch while they are still house-hunting or waiting on a chain to settle. In those cases, tell us the rough area you are aiming for and we will work with an estimate, then firm it up once you know. What we ask is that you are honest about how settled the plan is, because a quote built on a definite address is far tighter than one built on a maybe, and we would rather set the right expectation early.

sections.h: The Size and Type of Your Property
sections.body:
The single most useful thing you can give us is a clear sense of how much there is to move. The number of bedrooms is a handy shorthand, but it only goes so far. A two-bedroom house crammed after fifteen years of family life can hold more than a sparsely furnished four-bed. So go a little further: tell us roughly how furnished each room is, and crucially, mention the spaces people forget. Lofts, garages, sheds, conservatories and that cupboard under the stairs are where the hidden volume lives.

In our experience, the loft and the garage are the two areas that catch people out the most. They fill up quietly over the years, and on moving day they suddenly reveal a decade of boxes, tools and seasonal bits. If you flag them at the enquiry stage, we can plan the right van size and crew from the outset rather than scrambling on the day. The same goes for a home office full of equipment, a hobby room, or a kitchen with a serious amount of crockery. The more honest the picture, the closer the quote.

It also helps to tell us the type of property at both ends. A bungalow, a terraced house, a third-floor flat and a converted barn all present different challenges, and knowing the building helps us picture the job. We are not judging how much you own; we genuinely just need the real shape of the move so the figure we give you holds up when our crews arrive.

Access is the detail most people leave out, and it is one of the things that most affects how long a move takes. Tell us where the lorry can park at each address. Is there a driveway, or will we be working from the road? Is there a parking restriction, a permit zone, or a narrow lane where a large vehicle simply cannot reach the door? If everything has to be carried fifty yards from the nearest stopping point, that changes the day, and it is far better that we know now than discover it at eight in the morning.

Stairs matter too. A first-floor flat with no lift, a tight Victorian staircase, or a loft conversion reached by a steep, narrow flight all add time and call for the right approach. So do low doorways, gated developments, shared entrances and lifts that may or may not swallow a sofa. None of these are problems when we know in advance, because we can bring the right equipment and the right number of hands. They only become problems when they are a surprise.

Remember that access counts at both ends, and we plan around the harder of the two. A ground-floor pickup with parking outside the door means little if the new place is up two flights with a long carry. A quick note in your enquiry, something as simple as no driveway, lorry must use the road, and the new house has stairs to the front door, tells us a great deal and helps us price the move honestly the first time.

Some items deserve a specific mention because they shape both the plan and the price more than their number suggests. A piano is the classic example; it needs the right people, the right equipment and sometimes a different approach entirely, and it is not something to spring on a crew at the last minute. The same applies to large American-style fridge-freezers, heavy safes, full-size wardrobes that will not come apart, garden machinery, and anything genuinely weighty or fragile.

Antiques, glass cabinets, large mirrors, artwork and grandfather clocks all benefit from being flagged early so we can bring the proper protection and handle them with the care they deserve. If you have a particularly precious or unusual item, tell us about it and describe it as best you can. We have moved a great many treasured belongings over the years, and we would always rather plan for them properly than meet them unannounced and have to improvise on the day.

sections.h: Packing, Storage and Any Extra Help You Want
sections.body:
Tell us how much you would like us to do. Some customers pack everything themselves and just want us to load, drive and unload. Others want a full pack, where our crews wrap, box and protect the lot so they can simply hand over the keys. Many land somewhere in the middle, packing the books and clothes themselves while leaving the fragile kitchen and the awkward items to us. There is no wrong answer, but telling us your preference at the enquiry stage means the quote reflects the service you actually want.

Mention too whether you need any boxes and materials supplied, and whether you would like extras such as dismantling and reassembling beds and wardrobes, or disconnecting white goods. And if there is any gap between moving out and moving in, raise storage early. Being a family-run firm with our own storage here in Westerfield, we can fold a few days or a few weeks of secure storage neatly into the plan, which is far easier and far cheaper than arranging it in a panic when a completion date slips.

sections.h: Dates, Flexibility and the Best Way to Reach You
sections.body:
Give us your preferred moving date, or your best guess if the date is still moving around, which it often is in a chain. Tell us how firm it is, and let us know if you have any flexibility. Demand peaks at the end of the month when most completions land, around weekends and through the busy spring and summer, so if you can be a little flexible we can often find a slot that suits both your plans and your budget. Even knowing that you could go a day either way is useful to us.

Finally, tell us the best way and time to reach you, and the most straightforward way to arrange a survey, whether in person or by a quick video walk-through. The clearer your enquiry, the faster we can come back with an accurate, no-obligation quote, and the fewer rounds of questions we both have to go through. We are always happy to talk it through from scratch if you would rather, but a few well-chosen details up front genuinely make the whole thing smoother for everyone.

faqs: What information do you need to give me a removals quote?

At a minimum, your moving addresses or rough areas, the size and type of property, a sense of how much there is to move including lofts and sheds, any access or parking issues, large or special items, the help you want, and your preferred date. The more you tell us, the more accurate the quote.
Do I need a firm destination address to ask for a quote?

No. Many people enquire while still house-hunting or waiting on a chain. Just tell us the rough area you are aiming for and how settled the plan is, and we will give you an estimate, then firm it up once your destination is confirmed.
Why does access matter so much in my enquiry?

Access affects how long a move takes, and time is the main thing you are paying for. Stairs, parking restrictions, narrow lanes and long carries all add minutes that build into hours. Telling us in advance means we bring the right van, crew and equipment rather than meeting surprises on the day.
Should I mention items like pianos or antiques up front?

Yes, always. Heavy, fragile or unusual items such as pianos, safes, large appliances and antiques need the right people and equipment. Flagging them in your enquiry lets us plan and bring proper protection, rather than improvising on moving day.
